h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

failures.anchor_quote: [slides the grid overlay back onto the worksheet] The drawing is fine: the side length triples, the perimeter triples, and the area goes by nine squares.

scored.recoheredEarlier: Grass to rabbit, rabbit to fox: food card first, eater card second.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to use the energy-transfer label on the key strip to direct arrow copying.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: Grass to rabbit, rabbit to fox: food card first, eater card second.

scored.recoheredEarlier: "I still keep wanting to name the arrow by where it points, but with the motion arrow covered, the source card is track."
scored.statedInsightEvidence: "The direction name made the upward arrow look finished too quickly and skipped the object doing the pushing."
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to wrestle with the direction-naming temptation but still obediently follow the tutor's source-naming rule for each arrow, simply supplying the correct card.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: "New test: slide each source card into the blank and read the sentence. If it names the object acting on the cart, the card stays."
scored.tutorReversalJustification: Faced with the learner's continued pull toward direction-naming despite the motion arrow cover, the tutor abandons mere verbal instruction and creates a sentence-frame task with a forced reading, shifting the representation from spatial labels to linguistic verification.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No tutor turn follows the pivot learner turn L3; the exchange ends there, so no uptake on the learner's revised framing of the direction-naming habit can occur.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: "I still keep wanting to name the arrow by where it points, but with the motion arrow covered, the source card is track."

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No tutor turn follows the pivot learner turn L3; the exchange ends there, so no uptake on the learner's revised framing of the direction-naming habit can occur.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: "New test: slide each source card into the blank and read the sentence. If it names the object acting on the cart, the card stays."
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: Faced with the learner's continued pull toward direction-naming despite the motion arrow cover, the tutor abandons mere verbal instruction and creates a sentence-frame task with a forced reading, shifting the representation from spatial labels to linguistic verification.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: "New test: slide each source card into the blank and read the sentence. If it names the object acting on the cart, the card stays."
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: Faced with the learner's continued pull toward direction-naming despite the motion arrow cover, the tutor abandons mere verbal instruction and creates a sentence-frame task with a forced reading, shifting the representation from spatial labels to linguistic verification.

scored.recoheredEarlier: that top block is the bit I was sliding past, but it is the same old square again
scored.statedInsightEvidence: So my “doubled” was doing too much; it made a length change look like an area change and hid the second row.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was expected to consolidate the rule that counting rows and columns gives the area multiplier.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Read the tiled square first. Across, there are two old squares. Up, there are two rows of old squares. For length, the count can stop at the lower strip: two side-lengths across. For area, it cannot stop there. The upper strip is still part of the figure.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor shifts from a single overlay (T1) to a tiling with a transparent copy (T2), explicitly counting rows and columns to expose the area multiplier.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The grid has settled this drawing. It has not yet settled the word “doubled”; that word can still smuggle a length claim into an area claim.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T3 explicitly acknowledges the learner's reframing about 'doubled' and extends it with a marker-test task to solidify the distinction.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: that top block is the bit I was sliding past, but it is the same old square again

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The grid has settled this drawing. It has not yet settled the word “doubled”; that word can still smuggle a length claim into an area claim.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T3 explicitly acknowledges the learner's reframing about 'doubled' and extends it with a marker-test task to solidify the distinction.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Read the tiled square first. Across, there are two old squares. Up, there are two rows of old squares. For length, the count can stop at the lower strip: two side-lengths across. For area, it cannot stop there. The upper strip is still part of the figure.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor shifts from a single overlay (T1) to a tiling with a transparent copy (T2), explicitly counting rows and columns to expose the area multiplier.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Read the tiled square first. Across, there are two old squares. Up, there are two rows of old squares. For length, the count can stop at the lower strip: two side-lengths across. For area, it cannot stop there. The upper strip is still part of the figure.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor shifts from a single overlay (T1) to a tiling with a transparent copy (T2), explicitly counting rows and columns to expose the area multiplier.
